channel.title: Science
channel.description: The very best in news and research across the sciences.

item.title: Amid a flood of AI advances, astrophysicists are questioning the soul of their field
item.description: Researchers see enormous power in new tools—but also the potential end of astrophysics as a human endeavor

item.title: Memory reactivation underlies experience-dependent adaptive regulation of sleep | Science
item.description: Recent memories are consolidated during sleep by spontaneous reactivation. However, whether and how memory reactivation affects sleep dynamics remain unclear. By tracking and modulating memory activity during sleep in mice, we revealed that negative ...

item.title: Photocatalyzed oxidative cleavage of alkenes using CO2 as an oxygen donor | Science
item.description: Oxidative cleavage of carbon-carbon double bonds often requires hazardous reagents and demanding conditions. In this study, we report a photocatalytic oxidative cleavage of alkenes using benign carbon dioxide (CO2) as an oxygen donor, producing ketones ...

item.title: Homo cooperans: Understanding the nature of human cooperation | Science
item.description: Human cooperation is fundamental to solving collective challenges, yet its individual drivers remain insufficiently understood. Using globally representative data from an incentivized two-player cooperation experiment conducted in 125 countries (N = 101,...

item.title: Home alone: Remote work, isolation, and mental health | Science
item.description: How does remote work affect isolation and mental health? We drew on five nationally representative surveys of American workers (N = 588,322) conducted from 2011 to 2024, omitting the peak pandemic years of 2020–2021. Our difference-in-differences ...

item.title: Global extent and drivers of tree cover loss quantified with high-resolution satellite data | Science
item.description: Quantifying the drivers of tree cover loss globally provides a synoptic understanding of pressures on the world’s forests. Existing information about tree cover loss drivers relies on maps of coarse spatial and thematic resolution. In this study, we ...

item.title: Competition enables rapid adaptation to a warming range edge in a model plant community | Science
item.description: Most predictions of whether populations will adapt to warming range edges ignore species interactions. We experimentally tested whether range-edge populations can adapt to warming within a competitive, model plant community (Lemna spp. and Spirodela ...

item.title: The architecture of the internet creates risks for democracy | Science
item.description: Will democracy survive the internet? Do we need to choose between Facebook’s surveillance capitalism or democracy? Layered lines of evidence can inform questions like these. When considered together, the evidence gives rise to a concerning picture, as summarized in a recent report for the European Commission that I co-led.

item.title: Spontaneous problem-solving in bumble bees | Science
item.description: Problem-solving using novel solutions without explicit training is often considered a hallmark of cognitive flexibility. We investigated whether bumble bees (Bombus terrestris) could solve a novel object manipulation task spontaneously. Bees trained to ...

item.title: In Other Journals | Science
item.description: Editors’ selections from the current scientific literature

item.title: In Science Journals | Science
item.description: Highlights from the Science family of journals

item.title: Dual-use research under scrutiny | Science
item.description: Strengthened US oversight of dual-use research may face growing jurisdictional limits while imposing costs on domestic science

item.title: Aquavoltaics knowledge gaps undercut benefits | Science
item.description: Aquavoltaics systems, which integrate floating photovoltaics with aquaculture, are increasingly promoted as a synergistic solution for global energy and food security (1). Although this dual-use model can increase power efficiency and water quality (2), rapid industrial expansion is outpacing scientific understanding. To ensure sustainable development, aquavoltaics managers need more data about the effects of the systems, and policy-makers must establish standards and governance that adhere to scientific evidence.

item.title: Wine’s warning for farming in climate change | Science
item.description: In their Research Article “Rapid evolution predicts demographic recovery after extreme drought” (12 March, 10.1126/science.adu0995), D. N. Anstett et al. report that wild plant populations, such as Mimulus cardinalis, can recover from extreme drought, but only if they carry sufficient adaptive genetic variation before the crisis hits. The authors caution that their findings may not extend to species with lower genetic diversity and longer generation times. That caveat serves as a quiet warning for agriculture, given that most crops have no such lifeline. Wine grapes exemplify this vulnerability.

item.title: Effects of China’s underground infrastructure | Science
item.description: HomeScienceVol. 392, No. 6802Effects of China’s underground infrastructureBack To Vol. 392, No. 6802 Full accessLetter Share on Effects of China’s underground infrastructureQuan Jiang, Xu Guo, [...] , Shuo Wang, Hang Ruan, [...] , Shishu Zhang, and Chao Shi+3 authors +1 authors fewerAuthors Info & AffiliationsScience4 Jun 2026Vol 392, Issue 6802pp. 1029-1030DOI: 10.1126/science.aeh4973 PREVIOUS A…

item.title: Patently imperfect | Science
item.description: To expand access to medicines, two advocates call for rethinking intellectual property’s role in global health

item.title: A provocative view of evolution turns 50 | Science
item.description: The Selfish Gene continues to challenge readers half a century later

item.title: The lost social infrastructure of work | Science
item.description: Remote work has substantially increased social isolation, with measurable consequences for mental health

item.title: Ozone from wildfire plumes increases mortality | Science
item.description: Decades of improvement in surface ozone exposure in the United States risk being reversed by wildfires

item.title: Bad memories, bad sleep | Science
item.description: Sleep quality is regulated by the reactivation of memory cells

item.title: Ultraprocessed foods and obesity: Interpreting the evidence | Science
item.description: What do randomized controlled trials really tell us about the effects of ultraprocessed foods?

item.title: A narrowing window to understand AI | Science
item.description: As capabilities of artificial intelligence (AI) advance rapidly, human understanding of these systems is increasingly falling behind. Several trends are converging to make AI systems harder to understand just as they become more consequential. Without ...

item.title: After empty promises, string theory finds new uses
item.description: Physicists hope back-to-basics approach could point toward alternative paths to “theory of everything”

item.title: Radical proposal would block solar storms with orbital ‘airbag’
item.description: Gases released from satellites could slash the threat of severe space weather

item.title: Exclusive: NSF watchdog unit is no longer investigating research misconduct
item.description: New policy hands universities the reins in probing allegations against faculty

item.title: Brain mapping effort searches for roots of Parkinson’s and other diseases
item.description: Allen Institute initiative aims to capture earliest cellular changes and identify new drug targets

item.title: ‘That could be a game changer’: antiviral pill gets first test for Ebola prevention
item.description: Researchers are preparing to launch an unprecedented trial to protect people exposed to Ebola in the DRC and Uganda

item.title: White House seeks to tighten political oversight of grantmaking
item.description: Sweeping proposed rule, now open for comments, would also restrict foreign collaborations and remove federal funding for open-access fees

item.title: Brazil’s ‘rolling stone reefs’ teem with hidden diversity, DNA reveals
item.description: Vast offshore habitats face threats including mining, trawling, and drilling for oil

item.title: Severe obesity in human HFpEF alters contractile protein function and organization | Science
item.description: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) causes substantial morbidity and mortality and has few effective therapies. Its phenotype has changed over time, with morbid obesity and metabolic defects supplanting hypertension and cardiac ...
